channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: bust
item.description:
A bust in blackjack is a hand total above 21, which causes that hand to lose immediately under the standard rules [@thorp1966]. If the player busts, the dealer does not need to finish drawing. If the dealer busts after the player has stood, all remaining player hands that have not already busted win.

The immediacy of bust resolution shapes blackjack strategy. A player deciding whether to hit is not only asking how likely a better total becomes but also how likely the hand ends at once. That makes blackjack a threshold game: each added card is judged against the risk of crossing a hard upper limit, which is why blackjack strategy is organized around thresholds rather than long action sequences [@thorp1966].

item.title: double down
item.description:
To double down in blackjack is to double the original wager in exchange for committing to receive exactly one additional card [@thorp1966]. The action increases both risk and reward: the player invests more precisely when the expected value of one more card is favorable enough to justify the larger stake.

Double-down rules vary by ruleset. Some tables allow doubling on any first two cards. Others restrict it to totals such as 9, 10, or 11, or prohibit it after a split. Because doubling changes how much money enters favorable situations, its availability materially affects the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: hard hand
item.description:
A hard hand in blackjack is a hand whose total does not include an ace counted as 11 [@thorp1966]. Either the hand has no ace at all, or any ace it contains must count as 1 to avoid a bust. A 10-6 is a hard 16. Ace-6-10 is also a hard 17, because the ace can no longer remain 11.

Hard hands matter because they have less flexibility than soft hands. A hard 16 cannot absorb another high card without busting, while a soft 16 can. This is why blackjack strategy distinguishes sharply between hard and soft totals even when the visible number is the same [@thorp1966].

item.title: natural blackjack
item.description:
A natural blackjack in blackjack is a two-card total of 21 made from an ace and any ten-value card [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator]. It is structurally distinct from reaching 21 with three or more cards because casino rules usually pay it at a premium rather than at even money.

That premium matters mathematically. A game paying natural blackjacks at 3:2 is materially better for the player than a game paying them at 6:5, even if the rest of the visible procedure looks identical. This is one of the clearest examples of how a small rule change can shift the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: soft hand
item.description:
A soft hand in blackjack is a hand that contains an ace counted as 11 without causing a bust [@thorp1966]. For example, ace-six is a soft 17. If the player takes another card and would otherwise go over 21, the ace can convert from 11 to 1 and the hand becomes hard instead of busting.

Soft hands matter because they are more flexible than hard hands with the same visible total. A soft 17 can take another card without immediate failure, while a hard 17 cannot. This flexibility changes optimal decisions about hitting, standing, and doubling, and it is one reason dealer rules about soft 17 affect the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: split
item.description:
To split in blackjack is to divide a starting pair into two separate hands, each matched by an additional wager equal to the original bet [@thorp1966]. If the player is dealt two 8s, for example, they may split them and play each 8 as the base of a new hand.

Splitting matters because it can turn one weak composite hand into two stronger strategic positions. A pair of 8s totals 16, which is awkward as a single hard hand, but splitting creates two fresh 8-start hands with new decision paths. Rules about whether players may resplit, split aces, or double after splitting materially change blackjack strategy and expected value [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: surrender
item.description:
To surrender in blackjack is to concede the hand before play is completed and lose only part of the original wager, usually one half [@thorp1966]. Surrender exists because some starting positions are weak enough that cutting the loss is better than continuing.

Not all blackjack tables offer surrender, and the timing matters. Late surrender, where the option is available only after the dealer checks for a natural blackjack, is more favorable to the house than early surrender. Because surrender gives the player a controlled way to cap losses in especially weak positions, its presence lowers the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: upcard
item.description:
An upcard in blackjack is the dealer’s face-up card, revealed before the player makes decisions [@thorp1966]. It does not show the dealer’s full hand, but it provides the key public information the player uses to estimate the dealer’s likely finishing range.

Because the dealer follows a fixed drawing rule, the upcard anchors most blackjack decision-making. A dealer 6 suggests a higher chance of the dealer busting. A dealer ace suggests stronger completion outcomes and possible dealer blackjack. Basic strategy tables are organized around this relation between the player’s total and the dealer’s upcard [@thorp1966].
